title: How China's provinces power global trade
gist: 96% of China's nearly $1.2 trillion 2025 trade surplus originated from just four coastal provinces (Guangdong, Zhejiang, Jiangsu, Shandong), with Guangdong alone exceeding $1 trillion in trade value; China overtook the US in R&D expenditure in 2024, while Anhui/Hefei has emerged as the hub for China's 'New Three' exports (EVs, batteries, solar PV), and global trade is reconfiguring into regional blocs rather than deglobalizing.

themes.theme: Extreme concentration of China's trade surplus in four coastal provinces

insights.claim: 96% of China's nearly $1.2 trillion trade surplus in 2025 originated from just four provinces: Guangdong, Zhejiang, Jiangsu, and Shandong—all coastal, located at major river mouths that have historically connected Chinese manufacturing to global markets.
insights.speaker: World Economic Forum
insights.so_what: China's trade surplus is a coastal-province phenomenon; disruptions to these four provinces (weather, trade policy, logistics shocks) would disproportionately affect export performance and global supply chains.
insights.quote: 96% of the country's nearly $1.2 trillion trade surplus last year originated from just four provinces: Zhejiang, Guangdong, Jiangsu and Shandong.
insights.locator: In fact, 96% of the country's nearly $1.2 trillion

insights.claim: Guangdong alone recorded more than $1 trillion in trade value in 2025—more than one-fifth of China's national total—with smartphones, computers, and semiconductors as top exports; Hong Kong SAR remains its largest trading partner as a critical re-export hub.
insights.speaker: World Economic Forum
insights.so_what: Guangdong at $1T+ is effectively the world's largest trade province; Shenzhen-Dongguan manufacturing disruptions have direct global tech supply chain consequences disproportionate to their geographic footprint.
insights.quote: Guangdong, China's largest trading province, recorded more than $1 trillion in trade value in 2025, more than one-fifth of the nation's total.

insights.claim: 15 provinces recorded total trade exceeding $100 billion in 2025, while 8 of China's 31 province-level divisions recorded trade deficits—reflecting diverse roles as import centers, financial hubs, and resource allocators that collectively show China's trade system is more complex than a simple export machine.
insights.speaker: World Economic Forum
insights.so_what: China's trade story is diversifying beyond traditional coastal export hubs; understanding import-oriented provinces helps identify supply chain dependencies and premium market access opportunities.
insights.quote: Eight of Mainland China's 31 province-level divisions recorded trade deficits in 2025, reflecting their roles as centres for imports, finance, logistics and resource allocation
insights.locator: Eight of Mainland China's 31 province-level divisions

themes.theme: Emerging industrial provinces and the New Three product wave

insights.claim: Anhui province (especially its capital Hefei) has emerged as China's key center for the 'New Three' products—EVs, lithium-ion batteries, and solar PV—and is China's second-largest exporter of passenger vehicles, anchoring industrial upgrading beyond the traditional coastal provinces.
insights.speaker: World Economic Forum
insights.so_what: Anhui/Hefei is the fastest-growing strategic industrial cluster in China for green technology exports; auto, battery, and energy investors must monitor Hefei's production capacity trajectory.
insights.quote: Anhui, in particular its capital Hefei, has emerged as a key centre for the industries underpinning China's industrial upgrading, particularly the country's 'New Three' products: electric vehicles, lithium-ion batteries and solar photovoltaic products.

insights.claim: China overtook the US in gross domestic R&D expenditure in 2024, per OECD estimates—signaling that China's transition from cost-competitive manufacturer to innovation-driven exporter has crossed a definitive threshold.
insights.speaker: World Economic Forum
insights.so_what: China's R&D investment exceeding the US means technology export sophistication will continue rising structurally; industries dependent on assumed US or European technology leadership should reassess that assumption.
insights.quote: according to OECD estimates, China overtook the USA in gross domestic expenditure on research and development in 2024
insights.locator: according to OECD estimates, China overtook

themes.theme: Global trade reconfiguration and China+1 supply chain strategies

insights.claim: China's growing exports in EVs, batteries, and semiconductors have prompted many economies to incentivize 'China+1' supply chain diversification approaches, while China is simultaneously deepening trade ties with the Global South to offset headwinds from developed economies.
insights.speaker: World Economic Forum
insights.so_what: China+1 strategies are now structural, not cyclical; companies without credible dual-sourcing for strategic inputs from China face growing geopolitical risk premiums that increasingly affect financing and insurance costs.
insights.quote: Some are incentivizing or even requiring their companies to pursue supply chain diversification and 'China+1' approaches.
insights.locator: Some are incentivizing or even requiring

insights.claim: The result of global trade reconfiguration may not be less globalization but 'a reconfiguration of it'—trade increasingly organized through regional blocs, mini-lateral arrangements, and networks of trusted partners, creating a more fragmented and complex global trading landscape.
insights.speaker: World Economic Forum
insights.so_what: Trade fragmentation into blocs means companies and countries outside current regional networks face rising exclusion risk; building membership in multiple trade blocs is becoming a strategic necessity for multinationals.
insights.quote: Trade is increasingly being organized through regional blocs, mini-lateral arrangements and networks of trusted partners, creating a more complex and fragmented global trading landscape.
insights.locator: The result may not necessarily be less globalization
